content.text: Shared Claude platform libraries — the SDK wrappers, auth middleware, and prompt registry — can only remain high-quality if someone is explicitly accountable for each module. Without a formal stewardship model, libraries accumulate PRs nobody reviews, regressions nobody catches, and dependencies nobody upgrades.
The maintainer ownership model assigns three tiers of accountability —
— with explicit authority, responsibilities, and career progression paths for each.

attrs.alt: Maintainer ownership pyramid: Platform Core Team → Trusted Committers → Guest Contributors

content.text: The core team is the ultimate owner of every Claude platform library. Its members are full-time (or close to it) on platform work, own the release train, set the technical roadmap, and hold the SLA commitment for library uptime and review turnaround. A healthy core team has two to four engineers; beyond four, ownership becomes diffuse and accountability erodes.
Owns the release train: publishes patch, minor, and major versions on the documented cadence.
Sets the technical roadmap and accepts or rejects RFC proposals.
Holds the on-call rotation for production incidents that trace to a platform library.
Runs the quarterly trusted-committer nomination cycle.
Enforces the deprecation policy and communicates breaking changes.

Trusted committers are product-squad engineers who have demonstrated deep familiarity with a specific module and earned merge authority for it. They are the primary reviewers for guest-contributor PRs. A typical library has four to eight trusted committers spread across two to four squads, each owning one or two modules. Their time commitment is roughly 20% — enough to maintain a three-business-day review SLA without pulling them fully off product work.

# CODEOWNERS  — module-level trusted committer assignments
# Format: <path pattern>   @user-or-team ...

/src/acme_claude/auth.py          @alice @platform/core-team
/src/acme_claude/client.py        @bob @platform/core-team
/src/acme_claude/retry.py         @carol @dan @platform/core-team
/src/acme_claude/prompts.py       @eve @platform/core-team
/src/acme_claude/telemetry.py     @frank @platform/core-team
/evals/                           @platform/core-team
/docs/                            @any-trusted-committer

Every file in a platform library must have at least one trusted committer listed in CODEOWNERS. Unowned files are a review risk — PRs targeting them must be routed to the core team, which adds latency and violates the inner-source promise of timely feedback.

Any engineer at the company may contribute to platform libraries as a guest contributor. Guest contributors can open RFCs, submit pull requests, write documentation, and file bug reports. They do not have merge authority — every PR must be approved by a trusted committer before it can be merged. Consistent quality contributions are the path to trusted committer status.

The nomination cycle runs quarterly. Any trusted committer or core team member may nominate a guest contributor who has merged three or more PRs with positive review feedback. The nominee must not self-nominate.
Nominator opens a GitHub issue in the meta-repo using the trusted-committer nomination template.
All existing trusted committers for the relevant module have five business days to second or object.
Majority of seconds with no blocking objections proceeds to core team sign-off.
Core team approves and opens an onboarding PR: adds nominee to CODEOWNERS and grants GitHub team membership.
Nominee completes the trusted-committer orientation checklist (see below).

## Trusted Committer Orientation Checklist

**Repository access**
- [ ] Added to `@platform/trusted-committers-<module>` GitHub team
- [ ] Added to CODEOWNERS for owned modules
- [ ] Added to on-call rotation (shadow week before going primary)

**Knowledge transfer**
- [ ] Read Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) for owned module
- [ ] Reviewed last 6 months of merged PRs and their review threads
- [ ] Paired with outgoing reviewer for one review cycle

**Tooling**
- [ ] Can run `make release-patch` and `make release-minor` locally
- [ ] Can run `python evals/regression/run_evals.py --baseline main` locally
- [ ] Verified access to Semgrep and Bandit CI dashboards

**Communication**
- [ ] Joined #platform-claude-sdk Slack channel
- [ ] Joined weekly trusted-committer sync (Thursdays 10 AM)
- [ ] Introduced themselves in the #platform-announcements channel
Owning a module is more than approving PRs. Trusted committers and the core team have proactive stewardship duties that keep the library healthy between contributions.
Rotation audit: active vs inactive TCs

The platform engineering dashboard tracks per-module health weekly. Trusted committers receive a digest every Monday. Modules below the threshold on any metric trigger a remediation item in the next sprint.

# Run the weekly health report
python scripts/module_health.py --output slack
# Posts to #platform-claude-sdk-health with per-module status

# Sample output:
# ✅ auth.py        — 2 open PRs, 91% coverage, deps 12d
# ✅ client.py      — 0 open PRs, 88% coverage, deps 8d
# ⚠️  retry.py       — oldest PR 6d (SLA miss), coverage 84%
# ❌ prompts.py     — 14 open PRs, deps 60d overdue

Trusted committers who change teams, take extended leave, or become inactive (no review activity for 60 days) are off-boarded from the CODEOWNERS file and the GitHub team. The core team is responsible for identifying a replacement before the off-boarding is complete — a module must never be left without at least one active trusted committer.

## Trusted Committer Off-boarding Checklist

**Before departure**
- [ ] Complete all in-flight PR reviews or hand off to another TC
- [ ] Identify and brief a successor candidate to the core team
- [ ] Transfer ownership of any open ADRs or RFC issues

**Access removal (core team action)**
- [ ] Remove from CODEOWNERS
- [ ] Remove from `@platform/trusted-committers-<module>` GitHub team
- [ ] Remove from on-call rotation

**Knowledge preservation**
- [ ] Write a module runbook entry if none exists
- [ ] Record a 15-minute walkthrough video of non-obvious module invariants
- [ ] Update docs/trusted-committers.md with departure date and successor
Temporary backup TC from core team
Core team reaches out; off-board if no response
Core team assumes reviews; open emergency nomination
Maintainer ownership only works when communication is predictable. The following norms are enforced by the core team and expected of all trusted committers.
First response within 3 business days. This is a acknowledgment, not necessarily approval — 'I've read this, needs more context on X' satisfies the SLA.
Product Owner must respond to a new RFC within 5 business days. Silence is not approval.
Any Tier 3 change requires a 60-day advance notice posted to #platform-announcements and direct DMs to all teams consuming the library.
Every release — including patches — must have a CHANGELOG entry. Releases without CHANGELOG entries are blocked by the release workflow.
If a trusted committer cannot reproduce or fix a reported bug within 4 hours, they escalate to the core team on-call engineer.

# .github/workflows/release.yml  — CHANGELOG gate
name: Release
on:
  push:
    tags: ['v*']

jobs:
  validate-changelog:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Require CHANGELOG entry
        run: |
          VERSION="${GITHUB_REF_NAME#v}"
          if ! grep -q "## \[$VERSION\]" CHANGELOG.md; then
            echo "ERROR: No CHANGELOG entry for version $VERSION"
            echo "Add a ## [$VERSION] section to CHANGELOG.md before releasing."
            exit 1
          fi
Anti-Patterns and How to Avoid Them

Most inner-source programs that fail do so not because the contribution model was wrong, but because the stewardship model was neglected. The following anti-patterns are the most common causes of library decay in engineering organizations that have adopted inner-source without enforcing ownership norms.

Ghost ownership occurs when a trusted committer is listed in CODEOWNERS but has moved to a different team, taken on a new role, or simply stopped reviewing PRs. The CODEOWNERS entry still routes new PRs to them, but those PRs sit unreviewed for weeks. Contributors lose confidence in the inner-source model and stop submitting.
Prevention: the 60-day inactivity check runs automatically and pings the TC via Slack before escalating to the core team.
Detection: the weekly module health report flags any PR that has been waiting for a first response for more than 5 business days.
Remediation: core team reassigns the PR to an active TC and opens an off-boarding issue for the ghost owner.

# scripts/check_codeowner_activity.py
# Run weekly via cron to detect ghost owners before health degrades.
from github import Github
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

INACTIVE_DAYS = 60
g = Github()  # uses GITHUB_TOKEN from environment

repo = g.get_repo("acme/acme-claude-sdk")
# Load CODEOWNERS and extract unique GitHub handles
codeowners_content = repo.get_contents("CODEOWNERS").decoded_content.decode()
handles = set()
for line in codeowners_content.splitlines():
    if line.startswith("#") or not line.strip():
        continue
    for part in line.split()[1:]:
        if part.startswith("@") and "/" not in part:
            handles.add(part.lstrip("@"))

cutoff = datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(days=INACTIVE_DAYS)
inactive = []
for handle in handles:
    user = g.get_user(handle)
    # Check their review activity in this repo
    reviews = repo.get_pulls_review_comments(since=cutoff)
    reviewer_set = {r.user.login for r in reviews}
    if handle not in reviewer_set:
        inactive.append(handle)

if inactive:
    print(f"INACTIVE CODEOWNERS (>{INACTIVE_DAYS}d): {', '.join(inactive)}")

When too few trusted committers own too many modules, every PR queues behind the same two people. A module with a single trusted committer and active guest contribution can accumulate a six-week review backlog within a quarter. The fix is not to pressure the TC to review faster, but to run an early nomination cycle and bring in an additional TC before the bottleneck forms.
Rule of thumb: if a module receives more than four PRs per month, it needs at least two active trusted committers. If it receives more than ten, it needs three.

When a module's design decisions exist only in the heads of the original authors, new trusted committers cannot review effectively and guest contributors write PRs that conflict with hidden invariants. The fix is to write Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) for every non-obvious design choice and to keep them colocated with the code.

# docs/adr/0004-retry-jitter.md
# ADR 0004: Exponential Back-off With Jitter in retry.py

## Status
Accepted — 2024-03-15

## Context
The Anthropic API enforces per-minute rate limits at the workspace level.
When multiple services hit the limit simultaneously (thundering herd),
deterministic exponential back-off causes all retries to fire at the same
moment, amplifying the spike.

## Decision
Use full jitter: actual_delay = random.uniform(0, base_delay * 2^attempt).
This spreads retries across the back-off window and reduces re-hit probability
by ~60% in our load tests (see evals/retry-thundering-herd-2024-03.ipynb).

## Consequences
- Retry latency is non-deterministic; tests must use monkeypatch on random.
- Max observed retry delay is 2x the deterministic case in the worst 5th percentile.
- Do not change this to deterministic without re-running the load test.
Scaling the Model Across Multiple Libraries
As the Claude platform grows from one shared library to five or ten, the stewardship model must scale without requiring a proportional growth of the core team. Two practices make this feasible:
Each library maintains its own CODEOWNERS file and trusted committer set, but all TCs across all libraries are members of the

GitHub organization team. This allows the core team to broadcast policy changes and security notices to all TCs simultaneously, and lets TCs from one library assist with review overflow from another when needed.

Rather than each library tracking its own health metrics in isolation, the platform team maintains a single Grafana dashboard that aggregates module health across all inner-source repositories. Trusted committers can subscribe to per-module alerts so they are notified only when their modules need attention.
% TCs with review activity in 30 days
The maintainer ownership model gives every Claude platform library a clear chain of accountability: the
owns the release train and roadmap;
own review quality and module health for specific files;
drive feature velocity from across the organization.
Quarterly nomination cycles, explicit off-boarding checklists, and automated health dashboards prevent the model from drifting into a state where 'everyone owns it' means 'nobody owns it'.
Every file must have an entry in CODEOWNERS — unowned files are a review risk.
Trusted committers hold a 3-business-day review SLA and a 20% time commitment.
The nomination cycle is quarterly; three merged PRs with positive feedback is the baseline requirement.
Module health is tracked weekly: open PRs, coverage, dep age, eval freshness.
Off-boarding must complete before a TC's last day — a module is never left without an active owner.
Breaking changes require a 60-day advance notice; CHANGELOG entries are required for every release.
